title: The Out-of-Vocabulary Problem
concept: When a model encounters a word not seen during training, it fails; we handle this by mapping rare words to a special '<UNK>' token.

exercises.prompt: What is the term used when a machine learning model encounters a word it did not see during training?
exercises.explain: When an AI encounters a word it has never seen before, we say that word is 'Out-of-Vocabulary' (OOV) because it is outside the list of words the AI knows.

cards.title: When Robots Get Confused
cards.body: Imagine teaching a robot helper every word in the dictionary, but then someone says 'selfie' or 'fleek'—slang words it has never heard before. The robot freezes! That's the Out-of-Vocabulary (OOV) problem. Let's learn how to keep our models from freezing.

cards.title: What is the OOV Problem?
cards.body: The Out-of-Vocabulary (OOV) problem happens when an AI model meets a word it never saw during training. To fix this, we replace all unknown words with a special placeholder token, usually written as '<UNK>' (short for unknown).

cards.title: The Mystery Blank
cards.body: Imagine you are reading a book in a foreign language and come across a word you've never seen before. Instead of giving up completely, you write 'UNKNOWN' over it in pencil and keep reading to understand the rest of the sentence. The <UNK> token does the exact same thing for a computer!

cards.title: OOV in Action
cards.body:
If your vocabulary only knows: ['I', 'love', 'cats']

And you input: 'I love leopards'

The model translates 'leopards' (unseen) to '<UNK>'.

Your model processes it as: 'I love <UNK>' and can still understand the rest of the sentence!

cards.title: Do AIs Just Know Every Word?
cards.body: Many people think AI models are smart enough to guess the meaning of any new word instantly. In reality, basic models will completely crash or fail if they hit a word they haven't been programmed with, unless we use a backup plan like '<UNK>'.

cards.title: With vs. Without <UNK>
cards.body: See how a model processes a sentence containing an unknown word ('axolotl') depending on whether it has an OOV strategy.

left.text: Input: 'My axolotl swims'\nResult: Error! Word 'axolotl' is unknown. Processing stops.

right.text: Input: 'My axolotl swims'\nResult: 'My <UNK> swims'. Processing succeeds!

options.text: Words that were never seen during the model's training phase.
Words that are spelled incorrectly by the user.
Words that are too long for the computer to read.
Words that have multiple meanings like 'bark'.

exercises.explain: Out-of-Vocabulary (OOV) refers specifically to words that the model never encountered during its training phase, making them completely unknown to the model's vocabulary.

exercises.prompt: Using the '<UNK>' token helps the AI process sentences even if they contain words it has never seen before.
exercises.explain: By replacing unknown words with '<UNK>', the model can still read the rest of the known words in the sentence without crashing.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the token used to replace words that are not in the model's vocabulary.
exercises.sentence: When a model sees an unseen word, it replaces it with the blank token.
exercises.explain: The '<UNK>' token is the industry standard placeholder for replacing Out-of-Vocabulary (unknown) words during text processing.

exercises.prompt: A well-designed AI model encounters the unknown word 'retro'. Which of the following shows the AI handling this correctly?

options.text: The AI system crashes and stops working completely.
The AI replaces 'retro' with '<UNK>' and keeps reading.
The AI deletes the entire sentence because of one word.

exercises.explain: Replacing unseen words with '<UNK>' is a standard and effective way to handle OOV words. Deleting the entire sentence or crashing are bad designs.

exercises.prompt: If your AI model is reading the sentence 'I saw a platypus' but 'platypus' was never in its training data, how does it handle this word using the OOV solution?

options.text: The model automatically guesses its meaning based on spelling.
The model pauses training until you type in a definition.
It replaces the word with a special '<UNK>' token to keep going.
The model deletes the entire sentence containing that word.

exercises.explain: When using a simple vocabulary, any word that wasn't in your training set becomes a mystery. Mapping these unseen words to '<UNK>' prevents the model from crashing and allows it to process the rest of the text.
